data.name: Classic Espresso
data.description: The foundation of all espresso drinks. A properly pulled espresso is a thing of beauty: a rich, syrupy body topped with a layer of golden crema. This recipe focuses on the standard double shot, the benchmark every home barista should master before moving on to more complex drinks.
technique: Purge the group head with a short flush of water
Grind 18g of fresh coffee directly into the portafilter
Use a WDT tool to break up any clumps and distribute evenly
Tamp with firm, level pressure (about 30 lbs / 15 kg)
Lock the portafilter into the group head
Start extraction immediately
Target 36g of liquid espresso in 25-30 seconds
The stream should look like warm honey dripping from the spout
Stop the shot when you reach your target yield
tips: If the shot runs too fast (under 20 seconds), grind finer
If the shot runs too slow (over 35 seconds), grind coarser
Always adjust grind in small increments
Fresh beans (7-21 days post roast) produce the best crema
Taste first, then adjust. Sour = under-extracted, bitter = over-extracted
Pre-heat your cup with hot water for better temperature retention
pairsWith: A small glass of sparkling water to cleanse the palate

data.name: Caffe Latte
data.description: The latte is the crowd-pleaser of the espresso world. A double shot of espresso combined with silky steamed milk creates a smooth, approachable drink that highlights the sweeter, more caramel notes in your coffee. Mastering the milk texture is the key to a great latte, both for taste and for latte art.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into your cup
While the shot pulls, begin steaming 200ml of cold whole milk
Position the steam wand tip just below the milk surface
Introduce air for the first 2-3 seconds (you should hear a gentle hissing)
Submerge the tip to create a whirlpool, heating to 60-65°C
Tap the pitcher on the counter to pop any large bubbles
Swirl the pitcher to integrate the foam into a glossy, paint-like texture
Pour the milk into the espresso from a moderate height, then bring the spout close to the surface for latte art
tips: Cold milk gives you more time to texture properly
Whole milk produces the best microfoam due to its fat and protein content
The finished milk should look like wet white paint with no visible bubbles
For latte art, the milk needs to be poured within 10 seconds of finishing steaming
A 350ml pitcher is ideal for a single latte
Oat milk is the best dairy alternative for latte art

data.name: Cappuccino
data.description: The traditional Italian cappuccino features equal parts espresso, steamed milk, and milk foam. Compared to a latte, the cappuccino has more air incorporated into the milk, creating a lighter, airier texture with a more prominent foam cap. In Italy, cappuccinos are exclusively a morning drink, never ordered after 11 AM.
data.yield: 36g espresso + 120ml steamed milk with thick foam
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a 180ml cappuccino cup
Steam 120ml of cold whole milk
Introduce more air than for a latte: keep the tip near the surface for 4-5 seconds
You want about 1cm of foam on top of the milk in the pitcher
Continue heating with a whirlpool motion until 60-65°C
Tap and swirl the pitcher aggressively to break up any large bubbles
Pour the milk into the center of the espresso with a slight wiggle
The foam should sit proud above the rim of the cup
tips: The key difference from a latte is more air and less liquid milk
A proper cappuccino foam should hold a teaspoon of sugar on its surface for a moment
Use a smaller cup than a latte to maintain the proper ratio
Traditional Italian cappuccinos are 150-180ml total
Chocolate powder on top is optional and not traditional in Italy
The foam should be silky, not dry or stiff

data.name: Flat White
data.description: Originating from Australia and New Zealand, the flat white is the espresso-forward milk drink. It uses a ristretto double shot (a shorter, more concentrated extraction) combined with velvety microfoam that has very little air. The result is a drink where the espresso character shines through the milk, rather than being diluted by it.
technique: Pull a ristretto double (18g in, 27g out, 20-25 seconds) into a 180ml cup
Steam 150ml of cold whole milk with minimal air incorporation
Only introduce air for 1-2 seconds, just enough for a thin microfoam layer
Heat the milk to no more than 60°C for sweetness
The finished milk should be glossy with almost no visible foam
Pour directly into the center of the ristretto
The thin microfoam creates a flat, glossy surface, hence the name
Finish with a small latte art pattern if desired
tips: The ristretto shot is key. It should be sweeter and more intense than a standard shot
Less foam is the defining characteristic. Aim for almost no foam at all
Use a smaller cup (160-180ml) to keep the espresso-to-milk ratio strong
The lower milk temperature lets the coffee flavor come through
A flat white should taste noticeably more coffee-forward than a latte
This drink really rewards using high-quality, fresh beans

data.name: Pour-Over Coffee
data.description: Pour-over coffee is a meditative brewing method that gives you complete control over every variable. The slow, deliberate pouring of water over a bed of coffee produces a remarkably clean, nuanced cup that highlights the unique characteristics of single-origin beans. This recipe uses a standard V60-style dripper.
technique: Boil water and let it cool to 92-96°C (or use immediately off boil for light roasts)
Place a paper filter in the dripper and rinse thoroughly with hot water
Discard the rinse water from your server
Add 15g of medium-fine ground coffee to the filter
Bloom: pour 30-45g of water in a spiral, wetting all grounds evenly
Wait 30-45 seconds for the bloom (you should see bubbles forming as CO2 escapes)
First pour: slowly add water in concentric circles to 150g total (30 seconds)
Wait until the water level drops near the coffee bed
Second pour: add water to 250g total in concentric circles
Allow all water to draw through. Total time should be 2:30-3:30
tips: A gooseneck kettle is essential for controlling your pour rate and placement
Always rinse the paper filter to remove papery taste and preheat the dripper
Stale coffee will not bloom visibly. Use beans within 4 weeks of roasting
Adjust grind size based on total brew time: too fast means grind finer, too slow means grind coarser
Pour in slow, steady concentric circles, avoiding the edges of the filter
Light roast single-origin coffees tend to shine best with this method
The Arco Filtro grinder is optimized for pour-over grind consistency

data.name: Cold Brew Coffee
data.description: Cold brew coffee is steeped overnight in cold water, producing a smooth, low-acidity concentrate that is perfect for hot summer days. The long, slow extraction at room temperature or below draws out the sweeter, chocolatey notes while leaving behind much of the bitterness. Makes a concentrate that can be diluted to taste.
technique: Grind 100g of coffee on the coarsest setting
Add the ground coffee to a large jar or cold brew maker
Pour 500ml of filtered room-temperature water over the grounds
Stir gently to ensure all grounds are saturated
Cover and place in the refrigerator (or leave at room temperature for a bolder result)
Steep for 12-24 hours (16 hours is the sweet spot for most people)
Strain through a fine mesh filter, then through a paper filter for clarity
Store the concentrate in the fridge for up to 2 weeks
To serve, dilute 1:1 with cold water, milk, or pour over ice
requiredEquipment: Large jar or cold brew maker
tips: Use a medium to dark roast for the best cold brew flavor profile
A coarse grind prevents over-extraction during the long steep time
Refrigerator steeping produces a cleaner, milder result. Room temperature is bolder
The concentrate is strong. Always dilute before drinking
Cold brew naturally has about 60% less acidity than hot-brewed coffee
Add a pinch of salt to the concentrate to enhance sweetness and reduce bitterness
Try serving over coffee ice cubes (frozen cold brew) to prevent dilution

data.name: Americano
data.description: The Americano bridges the gap between espresso and drip coffee. By adding hot water to a double espresso, you get a long, sippable drink that retains the flavor complexity of espresso with a lighter, more approachable body. Legend has it that American soldiers in Italy during World War II diluted espresso to resemble the drip coffee they were accustomed to.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Heat 150-200ml of fresh water to 80-85°C (slightly below boiling to avoid scalding the espresso)
For a traditional Americano: add the hot water to the cup first, then pour the espresso on top. This preserves the crema
For a Long Black (Australian style): pour espresso directly into the hot water
Adjust the water amount to your preferred strength
Serve immediately
tips: The water temperature matters. Too hot and it scorches the espresso. 80-85°C is ideal
Adding espresso to water (Long Black style) preserves more crema on top
Use slightly less coffee than normal drip to avoid an overly intense flavor
An Americano from a good shot reveals flavors you might miss in straight espresso
Many machines have a hot water spout that can be used directly
This is a great drink for showcasing lighter roasted single-origin espressos

data.name: Cortado
data.description: The cortado is the perfect balance of espresso and milk. Originating from Spain (cortar means to cut), a cortado cuts the intensity of espresso with an equal measure of warm, lightly steamed milk. There is minimal foam, the milk temperature is lower than a latte, and the drink is served in a small 120-150ml glass. It is pure simplicity.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a small glass
Steam 60ml of milk just until warm (55-60°C), with almost no foam
Alternatively, heat milk gently in a saucepan without frothing
Pour the warm milk directly into the espresso
The result should be a 1:1 ratio of espresso to milk
Serve in a small cortado glass or tumbler
tips: The cortado is about simplicity. Do not overthink the milk
The milk should be warm, not hot. This keeps the drink drinkable in a few sips
There should be virtually no foam. Just warm, flat milk
This is an excellent drink for tasting different espresso beans
In many Spanish cafes, the cortado is the default coffee order
The Arco Double-Wall Glasses are perfect for cortados

data.name: Espresso Macchiato
data.description: The macchiato (meaning stained or spotted in Italian) is an espresso marked with just a small dollop of milk foam. It is the purists compromise: nearly a straight espresso, but with just enough milk to soften the edge. Do not confuse this with the sugary, flavored drinks that some chains have turned the name into. A true macchiato is just espresso with a spot of foam.
technique: Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) into a small espresso cup
Steam a small amount of milk (50ml) to create a thick, dense foam
Using a spoon, place a single dollop of foam on top of the espresso
The foam should float on the crema, creating a white spot on the brown surface
Serve immediately in an espresso cup
tips: The macchiato is 95% espresso, 5% milk. It should taste like espresso
Use a small spoon to place the foam precisely
Some baristas use a tiny pitcher to pour a dot of steamed milk instead
This is traditionally an afternoon drink in Italy
If you want slightly more milk, you are making a cortado instead
The foam acts as an insulator, keeping the espresso warm slightly longer

data.name: Affogato
data.description: Is it coffee? Is it dessert? The affogato (meaning drowned in Italian) is gloriously both. A scoop of cold vanilla gelato is drowned in a shot of hot espresso, creating a stunning contrast of temperatures, textures, and flavors. The espresso melts the gelato slowly, evolving from a frozen treat into a rich, creamy coffee drink. Pure Italian genius.
technique: Place a scoop of high-quality vanilla gelato or ice cream into a chilled glass or bowl
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Immediately pour the hot espresso directly over the gelato
Serve immediately with a spoon
Eat and drink as it melts, enjoying the evolving flavors and temperatures
tips: Quality gelato or ice cream makes all the difference. Use the best you can find
Vanilla is traditional, but hazelnut and pistachio gelato are incredible alternatives
A small glass keeps the portions in balance. Too much gelato overwhelms the espresso
Serve immediately. The beauty is in the contrast of hot and cold
For an adults-only version, add a splash of Amaretto or Frangelico
Use a slightly longer (standard ratio) shot rather than a ristretto for better balance with the sweet gelato

data.name: Caffe Mocha
data.description: The mocha is the indulgent crossover where coffee meets chocolate. A double espresso is combined with rich chocolate sauce and steamed milk, creating a drink that satisfies both coffee cravings and sweet tooth. Topped with whipped cream or chocolate shavings, the mocha is comfort in a cup and a gateway drink for those new to espresso-based beverages.
technique: Add 20g of high-quality chocolate sauce or melted dark chocolate to the bottom of a 240ml cup
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds) directly onto the chocolate
Stir vigorously to fully incorporate the chocolate into the espresso
Steam 150ml of cold whole milk to 60-65°C with a latte-like microfoam
Pour the steamed milk over the chocolate-espresso mixture
Optionally top with whipped cream and chocolate shavings or cocoa powder
Serve immediately
tips: Use real chocolate or high-quality chocolate sauce, not chocolate syrup, for the best flavor
70% dark chocolate provides the best balance with espresso
Stir the chocolate and espresso thoroughly before adding milk to avoid clumps at the bottom
For a white mocha, use white chocolate sauce instead
A mocha is an excellent choice for introducing espresso to people who find it too intense
For an iced mocha, use cold milk and pour everything over ice instead of steaming

data.name: Iced Latte
data.description: A refreshing iced latte combines the richness of espresso with the smooth coolness of cold milk over ice. The key to a great iced latte is pulling a concentrated, slightly shorter espresso that can stand up to dilution from the ice, and using enough ice to chill the drink instantly without watering it down over time.
technique: Fill a tall glass (350-400ml) to the top with ice
Pour 200ml of cold milk over the ice
Pull a double espresso (18g in, 36g out, 25-30 seconds)
Slowly pour the hot espresso over the milk and ice
The espresso will cool instantly and create beautiful layers
Stir to combine, or serve layered for visual effect
Optional: add simple syrup or flavored syrup before the milk
tips: Use plenty of ice. The espresso needs to be chilled rapidly to avoid a lukewarm drink
Cold milk poured first prevents the ice from melting too quickly
For a creamier result, use cold whole milk or oat milk
Pouring espresso over ice first (flash chilling) then adding milk is an alternative method
Coffee ice cubes prevent dilution as they melt
A slightly finer grind can compensate for the dilution from ice
